channel.title: DuckSquack
channel.description: Essays on AI, Technology & Society by Tim Hughes

item.title: The Board Resolves
item.description: Maps the emerging tripartite structure of global power: Musk's vertically integrated energy-compute-finance stack, Google's quietly indispensable Western infrastructure, and China's sovereign parallel civilisation, with orbital access as the unclaimed territory that may decide the age of abundant intelligence.

item.title: Nine Days Later
item.description: Tracks how war-driven disruption in the Strait of Hormuz exposed the petrodollar loop as physically fragile, accelerating global moves toward alternative energy and payment systems.

item.title: The Energy Epoch
item.description: Argues the petrodollar order is giving way to an energy-denominated era as AI, robotics, and solar drive intelligence, labor, and power costs toward zero, reshaping geopolitical winners and losers.

item.title: The Fixed Point
item.description: Reflects on world models and the strange loop where matter arranges itself into configurations that simulate the physics that produced it. Intelligence as the universe comprehending itself.

item.title: The Rustle in the Grass
item.description: A fifteen-year follow-up finding that warning signs have intensified and patterns of imperial decline have become more pronounced.

item.title: The Widening Gyre
item.description: Examines the growing divide between AI insiders and outsiders, warning that the gap in tacit knowledge compounds daily and may become unbridgeable.

item.title: Teaching Arthur
item.description: A father's curriculum for his son: practical skills, critical thinking, kindness with boundaries, and the lesson that you are stronger than you believe.

item.title: The Meaning Void
item.description: Asks what happens to meaning when work disappears, suggesting the answer may involve returning to older sources of identity: community, presence, and local contribution.

item.title: The Loneliness Dividend
item.description: Explores how loneliness has become an industry, with AI companions as the latest product that addresses the symptom while leaving the underlying causes untouched.

item.title: Digital Sovereignty
item.description: Examines Australia's dependence on foreign-controlled digital infrastructure, asking what sovereignty means when critical systems are owned by others.

item.title: The Chronic Patient and the Algorithm
item.description: Fifteen years of healthcare system failures examined through the lens of chronic illness, exploring how AI might fix informational failures while human presence remains irreplaceable.

item.title: The Last Normal Tuesday
item.description: Examines Australia's housing bubble, manufacturing collapse, and AI-driven job displacement, arguing that unprecedented economic disruption is imminent.

item.title: A Prosthetic for Empathy
item.description: Explores what happens when AI moves beyond imitating love to understanding its shape, and whether machines can extend human connection.

item.title: Escape Velocity
item.description: Argues that China has achieved structural advantages in AI development through manufacturing dominance, energy capacity, and civil-military integration that may prove insurmountable.

item.title: The System is Already Dead
item.description: A personal account of navigating systemic failure through chronic illness, arguing we must build for continuity rather than recovery.

item.title: What Does Australia Actually Make?
item.description: Catalogues Australia's manufacturing collapse and strategic vulnerabilities, arguing we have become a mine with a housing bubble attached through deliberate policy failure.

item.title: Sandpapering Our Structural Decay
item.description: Critiques Australia's misplaced priorities, comparing national outrage over cricket cheating to indifference toward economic decline and squandered natural resources.

item.title: Parenthood in the Age of Super Intelligence
item.description: Reflects on becoming a father during the AI revolution, exploring how to prepare a child for a world where traditional education and work are being transformed.

item.title: After Scarcity, Mastery
item.description: Envisions a post-scarcity future where survival is automated and meaning is found in craft and excellence for its own sake. Proposes the foundations needed: closed-loop manufacturing, peace infrastructure, and stable capital.

item.title: Reverse Engineering Nature's Quantum
item.description: Explores how quantum phenomena already pervade biology, suggesting we are catching up to computational processes that have existed for billions of years.

item.title: Navigating the MAD MAP: Mutual Assured Destruction vs. Mutual Assured Prosperity
item.description: Presents two possible AI futures: Mutual Assured Destruction through conflict and fragmentation, or Mutual Assured Prosperity through cooperation.

item.title: The Inheritance Cliff
item.description: Examines how parental wealth has become the primary determinant of home ownership in Australia, arguing we have constructed a de facto aristocracy while maintaining democratic forms.

item.title: The Death of the Junior Role
item.description: Argues that AI is eliminating entry-level positions across professions, severing the apprenticeship pipeline through which expertise has traditionally been transmitted.

item.title: The Boy and the World
item.description: I have been thinking about fatherhood. Not in the abstract sense that people mean when they say they want children someday, the way they say they w...

item.title: The Comfortable Dependency
item.description: I want to map something. Not a geographic map but a dependency map. A diagram of what Australia depends on, who provides it, and what happens if th...

item.title: The Other Moore's Law
item.description: Something is happening in Chinese artificial intelligence that most Western observers have not fully registered. I want to describe what I see, as ...

item.title: What the Model Sees
item.description: A machine generated a photograph this week. Not a photograph in the traditional sense, no light passed through a lens, no sensor captured a scene, ...

item.title: What Survived the Crash
item.description: FTX collapsed in November. Sam Bankman-Fried, who had been on the cover of magazines and testifying before Congress and donating millions to politi...

item.title: The Gap Between the Map and the Territory
item.description: I want to talk about a specific kind of dishonesty. Not the kind where someone says something they know to be false. The kind where an entire socie...

item.title: The Discontinuity
item.description: I have spent two weeks with ChatGPT. I am writing this in December, three weeks after OpenAI released it to the public, and I want to set down what...

item.title: The Feudal Return
item.description: I have a friend who earns a hundred and twenty thousand dollars a year. He is thirty-one, works in financial services, saves diligently, and lives ...

item.title: The Refinery That Closed
item.description: The ExxonMobil refinery at Altona closed last year. I want to trace what happened next, because the closure itself was a headline and what followed...

item.title: Thirty
item.description: I turned thirty this week. People turn thirty every day. But I have been sitting with it, and what I find when I sit with it is not nostalgia or re...

item.title: The Epistemological Crisis
item.description: People no longer disagree about what to do. They disagree about what is real. I want to dwell on this distinction because I think it is the most im...

item.title: Energy as Leverage
item.description: Russia invaded Ukraine three weeks ago. In the time since, I have watched Europe discover something that should not have required a war to reveal: ...

item.title: The God-Shaped Hole
item.description: Two years of isolation. Two years in which the thin structures of communal life, the office, the pub, the gym, the casual encounter on the street, ...

item.title: Inflation Is a Memory
item.description: Prices are rising. The CPI ticked up this quarter in ways that made economists uncomfortable, though the discomfort was expressed in the careful la...

item.title: The Oasis Is Coming
item.description: Facebook rebranded as Meta last month. The internet has responded with the energy it reserves for its favourite targets: mockery, memes, the comfor...

item.title: The Island Factory
item.description: There is a factory in Hsinchu, Taiwan, that you have never heard of and that your life depends on. It is operated by the Taiwan Semiconductor Manuf...

item.title: Kabul
item.description: The photographs from Kabul this week are the kind that do not require commentary. They provide their own. People clinging to the fuselage of a C-17...

item.title: The Wealth We Walk Past
item.description: Iron ore hit $230 a tonne this month. I want to sit with that number for a moment, because the number itself is less important than what it represe...

item.title: Controlled Substances
item.description: Melbourne is in its sixth month of lockdown. The longest in the world. I am in Adelaide, where the restrictions have been less severe but the atmos...

item.title: The Burning Library
item.description: A man I met last year, at a manufacturing conference I attended out of curiosity rather than professional obligation, told me he was one of the las...

item.title: The Zoom Class
item.description: There are two pandemics. They happen in the same country, in the same cities, at the same time, but they are experienced by different people in way...

item.title: The Argument from Pain
item.description: Three o'clock in the morning. Adelaide is silent. The house is dark. The only thing that exists is the pain, which has settled behind my right eye ...

item.title: The Money Printer and the Emperor
item.description: The Federal Reserve has printed approximately three trillion dollars since March. The Reserve Bank of Australia has launched its first-ever quantit...

item.title: The X-Ray
item.description: The pandemic did not break anything. This is the thing I keep coming back to, the observation that I think gets lost in the urgency of the crisis r...

item.title: The Summer That Came Early
item.description: The fire season started in September. This is not normal. Fire season in southeastern Australia begins, in the pattern most of us grew up with, in ...

item.title: The Credential and the Skill
item.description: A friend of mine graduated recently with a degree in information technology from a well-regarded Australian university. Three years of study, forty...

item.title: The Narrow Strait
item.description: I have been looking at maps lately. Not the political maps that show borders and capitals and the colourful fiction of national sovereignty, but th...

item.title: The Salary and the House
item.description: The median house price in Sydney crossed one million dollars this year. In Melbourne it is heading the same direction. Adelaide, where I live, rema...

item.title: The Unelected Infrastructure
item.description: Cambridge Analytica is the scandal. What lies beneath it is the revelation, and the revelation is bigger than the scandal. The scandal is that a po...

item.title: Why Civilisations Forget How to Build
item.description: The Sydney light rail project was supposed to cost $1.6 billion and take four years. It will cost over $3 billion and take considerably longer. The...

item.title: The Algorithm Knows You Better
item.description: Something happened this year that I think deserves more attention than it received, though it received quite a lot. Facebook's advertising platform...

item.title: The Last Factory
item.description: Ford closed first. Then Holden announced. Then Toyota. By the time you read this, the last car will have rolled off the last Australian production ...

item.title: The Quiet Abdication
item.description: I have been watching the American alliance from Adelaide with the particular attention of someone whose national security depends on it. The attent...

item.title: The Rerun of Rome
item.description: A 2011 essay comparing American structural patterns to late Rome: currency debasement, military overextension, infrastructure decay, and declining civic virtue.
